title: My Cat Drank Coffee: What to Do and Symptoms to Watch For
meta_desc: If your cat drank coffee, learn the risks of caffeine toxicity, when to call a vet, and how to triage this medium-urgency situation at home.
content_html:
TL;DR: Caffeine is toxic to cats; while a tiny lick of coffee is typically not harmful, ingesting concentrated coffee, grounds, or additives like Xylitol requires immediate veterinary attention.

What are the risks to my cat if they drink coffee or consume caffeine?

Coffee contains caffeine, which is a stimulant belonging to the methylxanthine family. While caffeine is a daily ritual for many humans, cats are significantly more sensitive to its effects. Even small amounts can overstimulate a cat's central nervous system and heart, leading to potential toxicity depending on the volume and concentration of the coffee ingested.

Is it an emergency if my cat drank coffee, and when should I call a vet?

  • The urgency level for a cat ingesting coffee is classified as Medium.
  • A single lick of diluted coffee from a mug is usually not cause for panic.
  • Contact your veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately if your cat has ingested more than a teaspoon, concentrated espresso, or dry coffee grounds.
  • Watch for symptoms like restlessness, vomiting, or a racing heart, which typically appear within one to two hours.

How does providing a photo of the coffee container help with my cat's triage?

  • A photo of the mug or packaging allows professionals to estimate the total volume of liquid your cat might have consumed.
  • Label photos are vital for identifying additives like sugar-free creamers or syrups that may contain Xylitol—a substance far more toxic to pets than caffeine.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

If a cat has ingested coffee, which contains methylxanthines like caffeine, prompt veterinary intervention is crucial. For recent ingestions (within 1 hour) and in the absence of clinical signs, induce emesis using apomorphine or hydrogen peroxide. Gastric lavage may be considered in sedated animals. Administer activated charcoal (1-4 g/kg, PO) and repeat every 12 hours in symptomatic animals due to enterohepatic recirculation of methylxanthines, controlling vomiting with metoclopramide (0.2-0.4 mg/kg, SC or IM, qid, as needed). Monitor cardiac status via electrocardiography, place a urinary catheter to prevent reabsorption across the bladder wall, and correct any acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities, as clinical signs may persist for up to 72 hours in severe cases. For tremors or mild seizures, administer diazepam (0.5-2 mg/kg, slow IV); barbiturates may be required for severe seizures. Treat arrhythmias as needed: propranolol (0.02-0.06 mg/kg, slow IV) or metoprolol (0.2-0.4 mg/kg, slow IV) for tachyarrhythmias, atropine (0.01-0.02 mg/kg) for bradyarrhythmias, and lidocaine (1-2 mg/kg, IV, followed by 25-80 mcg/kg/min infusion) for refractory ventricular tachyarrhythmias. Fluid diuresis may assist in stabilizing cardiovascular function and hastening urinary excretion of methylxanthines.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency, Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2967)

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of caffeine poisoning in cats?
faqs.answer: Common signs include hyperactivity, restlessness, rapid breathing, tremors, vomiting, and an elevated heart rate. In severe cases, it can lead to seizures.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat milk to dilute the coffee?
faqs.answer: No, do not offer milk as many cats are lactose intolerant and this may cause stomach upset, making it harder for the vet to determine if symptoms are from the coffee or the milk.

faqs.question: How long does caffeine stay in a cat's system?
faqs.answer: Caffeine is absorbed quickly and symptoms can last for several hours. Most cats will require monitoring or professional treatment for 6 to 12 hours depending on the amount ingested.
